title: Auto-Forwarding: Azure Service Bus's Most Under-Appreciated Feature
description: Why giving your downstream teams true ownership of their queues is the secret to scalable, drama-free message routing.
content:
For months, my morning routine started the same way: I’d check Slack and find a few requests from other teams asking me to manually resubmit their dead-lettered messages. I was managing a central Service Bus namespace - nothing massive, just 3 topics with about 7 subscriptions each - but I had become a bit of a messy bottleneck.

The Shared Infrastructure Friction

When you have multiple teams reading directly from your Subscriptions, you hit two walls immediately: Alarms and Permissions.

Because the lowest-level entity for many Azure Monitor metrics is the Topic itself, all the 'Subscription DLQ' alerts were routing to me. It wasn't a life-or-death crisis, but it was annoying to be the designated 'janitor' for bugs in codebases I didn't even own. Meanwhile, the consumer teams were frustrated because I couldn't give them Data Owner permissions on the production Topics - which is required if you want a team to be able to resubmit their own messages.

The "AWS Gold Standard" (in Azure)

In the AWS world, the SNS-to-SQS pattern is the gold standard. You never read from an SNS topic directly; you point it at an SQS queue. In Azure, we have the exact same capability, but it’s hidden under a property called ForwardTo. When you enable this, the Subscription stops being a mailbox and starts being a router. It catches the message and immediately flings it into a dedicated Queue owned by the consumer team.

The Strategic Win: Domain Sovereignty

By switching to Subscription-to-Queue auto-forwarding, the architecture changes from 'Shared Infrastructure' to 'Scoped Ownership.'

  1. Scoped Permissions (The Security Win): You can give the consumer team Service Bus Data Owner access to only their specific Queue. They can retry messages, purge their DLQ, and inspect payloads without you ever needing to grant them permissions on the central Topic.
  2. Alarm Sovereignty: Their DLQ depth alarms are now scoped to their specific Queue. The alerts bypass the publisher entirely and go straight to the team that actually owns the consumer.
  3. Safe, Isolated Replays: If a consumer team fixes a bug, they can resubmit dead-lettered messages directly back onto their Queue. The message is processed successfully without being broadcast to any other subscriber on the Topic.

Writing the Infrastructure as Code

Implementing this pattern is a one-line change in your templates. It's essentially the 'SNS-to-SQS' pattern for Azure developers.

Bicep Implementation

// The Topic Subscription (The Router)
resource subscription 'Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/topics/subscriptions@2022-10-01-preview' = {
  name: 'sub-consumer-a'
  parent: topic
  properties: {
    // This is the magic line that enables ownership:
    forwardTo: targetQueue.name
  }
}

The One Error You Need to Know

One tricky aspect of auto-forwarding is debugging routing failures. If the destination queue is full, disabled, or deleted, messages land in the Subscription's Dead Letter Queue with a DeadLetterReason of TransferEndpointUpdateFailed.

If you see this error, it means your 'pipe' is broken before the message even reached the consumer's 'bucket'.

🔍 Monitoring with Bussin
Tracking down these TransferEndpointUpdateFailed errors in the Azure Portal is a nightmare of blade-clicking. I built Bussin specifically to make this topology obvious. It visually flags auto-forwarders in the list and surfaces both the subscription-level and queue-level DLQs on a single screen so you never lose track of a message between teams.

title: How to Use Bussin: A Complete Guide
description: Bussin is a free, browser-based Azure Service Bus management tool. This guide walks you through connecting your first namespace to advanced message management — no installation needed.
content:
Step 1: Sign In with Microsoft

Click Sign in with Microsoft on the home page. You'll be redirected to Microsoft's login, where you sign in with the same account you use for the Azure Portal. Bussin never sees your password.

Step 2: Grant Consent

After logging in, Bussin requests two permissions:

  • Azure Service Bus (Data Plane) — to peek, send, and manage messages
  • Azure Management API — prompted separately, to list your namespaces automatically

Both are standard Azure API scopes. You can revoke them any time at myapplications.microsoft.com.

Step 3: Choose a Namespace

Bussin auto-discovers all Service Bus namespaces across your subscriptions. Use the search bar to filter by name, resource group, or subscription. Click a namespace to open it.

Step 4: Browse Queues and Topics

Once inside a namespace, you'll see your queues and topics in the left panel. Click one to load its message count, dead-letter queue depth, and other stats in real-time.

Step 5: Peek Messages

Use the Peek button to view messages without consuming them. Bussin supports iterative peeking of up to 10,000 messages. You can filter the results by message body, properties, or sequence number.

Step 6: Send, Purge & Resubmit

  • Send — Compose and send test messages, including batch JSON uploads
  • Purge — Clear all messages from a queue or dead-letter queue
  • Resubmit — Move dead-letter messages back to the main queue
💡 Tip: Try Demo Mode first
Not ready to connect a real namespace? Click Run Demo on the login page for a fully featured sandbox with no login needed.

title: Mastering Background Tasks: High-Performance Operations
description: How Bussin uses background workers to handle massive Service Bus operations like purging and searching without locking your UI.
content:
When handling thousands or millions of messages in Azure Service Bus, synchronous operations are a disaster. They lock your browser and provide zero feedback. Bussin solves this with a robust background task system.

Why Background Tasks?

Blazor WebAssembly runs in a single-threaded environment (the browser's UI thread). If you try to delete 1,000,000 messages in a single loop, your browser will freeze, and your users will see a 'Page Unresponsive' error. Bussin offloads these long-running operations to a background worker system that keeps the UI smooth.

Purging at Scale

Need to clear a dead-letter queue with 500,000 messages? In most tools, this is a slow, manual process. In Bussin, you hit Purge, confirm the operation, and then carry on with your other work.

Once started, the task appears in the bottom-left corner of your screen. You can see exactly how many messages have been cleared in real-time.

The Event Log

Every background task reports its progress and eventual success or failure to the Event Log. This acts as your audit trail for what happened in your namespace during a debugging session.

🔍 Power User Tip: You can run multiple background tasks simultaneously. You could be purging one queue while searching another and peeking a third.

title: The Best Azure Service Bus Explorer Alternatives in 2026
description: Comparing the best Azure Service Bus management tools in 2026: Bussin, Serverless360, QueueExplorer, and more. Find the right tool for your workflow.
content:
Why Look for an Alternative?

The original Service Bus Explorer is Windows-only, requires local installation, and its UI hasn't kept pace with modern standards. As teams move to macOS, Linux, and cloud-first workflows, better options have emerged.

1. Bussin — Best Free Web-Native

Bussin is a zero-backend, browser-native explorer. It uses Entra ID (Azure AD) and AMQP-over-WebSockets to connect directly from your browser to Azure. It's the only tool that requires zero installation and zero credentials storage.

  • ✅ Price: Permanently free and open source
  • ✅ Platforms: Web-native (Chrome, Edge, Safari, Firefox)
  • ✅ Highlight: No connection strings needed; handles 10k+ message peeks with streaming filtering.

Try Bussin now →

2. Serverless360 (Best for Enterprise)

Formerly known as ServiceBus360, this is a massive enterprise-grade observability platform. It goes far beyond a simple explorer, offering governance, detailed monitoring, and automated remediation for Azure messaging services.

  • ✅ Price: Paid (Enterprise licensing)
  • ✅ Platforms: Web-based SaaS
  • ✅ Highlight: Sophisticated monitoring and business activity tracking.

3. QueueExplorer (Best Desktop UI)

A high-performance desktop application for Windows that manages not just Service Bus, but also MSMQ, RabbitMQ, and ActiveMQ. It's built for power users who handle thousands of messages daily and need local speed.

  • ✅ Price: Paid (Per-user license)
  • ✅ Platforms: Windows
  • ✅ Highlight: Extremely fast localized search and bulk message editing.

4. Service Bus Explorer (The Original)

The open-source tool that started it all. If you are on Windows and don't mind the dated UI, it is still the most feature-complete tool available, supporting advanced operations like namespace cloning and complex rules management.

  • ✅ Price: Free (Open Source)
  • ✅ Platforms: Windows
  • ✅ Highlight: Nearly 100% feature coverage of the Service Bus API.

5. Azure Portal (Built-in)

Microsoft has improved the built-in Service Bus Explorer in the Azure Portal significantly. It's the best option when you are on a restricted machine where you can't access external tools.

  • ✅ Price: Free (Built-in)
  • ✅ Platforms: Web
  • ✅ Highlight: Always available; zero configuration; natively integrated with Azure Monitor.
Verdict: If you need enterprise monitoring, Serverless360 is the standard. If you want a fast, free, modern way to manage messages without installing anything, Bussin is the winner.

title: Security & Permissions: How Bussin Handles Your Data
description: A detailed look at the zero-backend architecture, consent flows, and why your credentials never leave your browser.
content:
The Short Answer

Your credentials never touch Bussin's infrastructure. There is no Bussin server. When you use Bussin, your browser talks directly to Microsoft and Azure.

Zero-Backend Architecture

Bussin is a Blazor WebAssembly application — it compiles to JavaScript and runs entirely inside your browser. There's no API server, no message proxy, no database. The app is just static files served from a CDN.

This means:

  • No Bussin server can log your messages
  • No credentials are stored server-side
  • There is no account system — Bussin doesn't know who you are

How Authentication Works

Bussin uses MSAL.js (Microsoft Authentication Library) to authenticate you via Entra ID. Here's the flow:

  1. You click Sign in with Microsoft
  2. Your browser redirects to login.microsoftonline.com — Microsoft's servers
  3. You authenticate with your Microsoft account (MFA applies as normal)
  4. Microsoft returns an OAuth 2.0 access token to your browser
  5. That token is stored in your browser's session storage only

Bussin receives the token but never transmits it to any external server. It uses it to sign API calls sent directly to Azure.

What Permissions Are Requested?

Bussin requests two OAuth scopes:

  • https://servicebus.azure.net/user_impersonation — the Azure Service Bus Data Plane API. This lets Bussin peek, send, and manage messages in namespaces you already have access to.
  • https://management.azure.com/user_impersonation — the Azure Management API. This lets Bussin discover your namespaces automatically, so you don't have to type connection strings.

Both use your existing Azure RBAC permissions. Bussin cannot access any resource you wouldn't already have access to in the Azure Portal.

AMQP over WebSockets

After authentication, message operations use the Rhea AMQP library to open a secure WebSocket connection to your Service Bus namespace. The connection goes from your browser directly to <namespace>.servicebus.windows.net — no intermediary.

Revoking Access

You can revoke Bussin's consent at any time from myapplications.microsoft.com under Apps & Services.

🔍 Open Source
You don't have to take our word for it. Bussin is fully open source. You can inspect the authentication code and message handling on GitHub.

title: Bussin Power User Tips & Tricks
description: Namespace filtering, iterative 10k-message peeking, PWA install — tips that change how you debug Service Bus.
content:
1. Type Anywhere to Filter

On the namespace selection screen, just start typing — you don't need to click the search box first. Bussin filters across namespace names, resource groups, and subscriptions simultaneously.

2. Install as a PWA

If you're using Chrome or Edge, look for the install icon in the address bar. Installing Bussin as a Progressive Web App gives you standalone windows and taskbar integration.

3. Peek 10,000 Messages Without Freezing

Bussin uses an iterative peeking strategy — it fetches messages in batches and streams them into the UI. You can start filtering and reading while the rest load. The Azure Portal caps you at 32; Bussin lets you go up to 10,000.

4. Filter After Peeking

Once messages are loaded, the inline filter narrows by message body content, MessageId, or Subject. Everything runs locally in your browser, so it's instant.

5. Batch Send from JSON

Need to replay a set of test messages? Prepare a JSON array and upload it via the batch send feature. Bussin parses each element as a message body and sends them all to the queue.

6. Dead-Letter + Resubmit Workflow

The fastest dead-letter debugging workflow in Bussin: Peek messages to understand failure, fix code, then hit Resubmit to move them back to the active queue.

7. Demo Mode for Onboarding

Use Run Demo to walk a new team member through Bussin without giving them Azure access. The demo environment is fully interactive.

8. Reverse Peek (LIFO)

Need to see the newest messages first? Enable Reverse peek (LIFO) in Advanced Peek. Bussin calculates the starting sequence number from the entity's message count and peeks backward — great for checking what was just sent.

9. Dark Mode

Click the moon/sun icon in the bottom-left of the sidebar to toggle dark mode. Your preference is saved in local storage and persists across sessions.

10. Entity Status Icons

In the entity selector, look for icons next to queue and subscription names. They tell you at a glance whether an entity is session-enabled, partitioned, has duplicate detection, or uses auto-forwarding — without opening the Azure Portal.

💡 Got a tip we missed?
Open a GitHub Discussion at github.com/sgebb/bussin and share it!

title: Azure Service Bus Best Practices
description: Queues vs Topics, Managed Identity, Dead-Letter Queues, and message patterns for resilient distributed systems.
content:
Topics vs Queues: Choose for the Future

A Queue delivers each message to exactly one consumer. A Topic with subscriptions delivers each message to multiple consumers independently.

Use topics even if you only have one subscriber today. Adding a second consumer later is easy. Migrating from a queue to a topic later requires changing your producer code.

Use Managed Identity — Not Connection Strings

Connection strings are a shared secret. Use Managed Identity and Azure RBAC instead. No secrets to rotate or leak. Assign narrow roles like Data Sender or Data Receiver specifically.

Always Handle Dead-Letter Queues

Every queue and subscription has a Dead-Letter Queue (DLQ). Monitor your DLQs. An overflowing DLQ is usually the first sign of a bug in production. Use Bussin to peek and resubmit DLQ messages during debugging.

Set Message TTL Intentionally

The default 14 days can be too long. Set a TTL that reflects how long a message is still useful. For many scenarios, that's minutes or hours.

Use Sessions for Ordered Processing

If you need messages to be processed in order, enable session mode. Service Bus guarantees FIFO order within a session.

Auto-Forward for Fan-Out Patterns

Use auto-forwarding to chain entities. A common pattern: create a subscription per downstream service, with each subscription auto-forwarding to a dedicated queue.

See it in action:
Connect Bussin to your namespace to browse queues, topics, and subscriptions and verify your configuration matches what you expect.

title: Dead Letter Queue Debugging: A Complete Workflow
description: How to debug Azure Service Bus dead letter queues efficiently. Peek DLQ messages, inspect failure reasons, fix your code, and resubmit — all from the browser.
content:
What Ends Up in the Dead Letter Queue?

Messages land in the DLQ for several reasons:

  • Max delivery count exceeded — the consumer threw an exception too many times
  • TTL expiration — the message sat in the queue longer than its time-to-live
  • Filter evaluation errors — a subscription filter couldn't evaluate the message
  • Manual dead-lettering — your application explicitly dead-lettered the message

Whatever the cause, the messages are sitting in the DLQ with their original bodies and properties intact, plus extra dead-letter metadata like DeadLetterReason and DeadLetterErrorDescription.

Step 1: Toggle DLQ View

In Bussin, select your queue or topic subscription, then click the DLQ toggle button in the actions sidebar. The message counts update immediately — you'll see how many dead-lettered messages are waiting.

Step 2: Peek DLQ Messages

Use Peek or Advanced Peek to inspect the messages. Since these are dead-lettered, they won't be consumed — peeking is always safe. You can peek up to 10,000 DLQ messages at once using Bussin's iterative peeking.

Once loaded, use the inline filter to search by message body content, MessageId, or Subject. If your DLQ has more than 10,000 messages, use Deep Search mode to scan up to 1 million messages in the background.

Step 3: Inspect Message Details

Click any message to open the detail modal. You'll see:

  • Broker properties — Sequence number, enqueued time, delivery count, TTL, content type, correlation ID, session ID, partition key, and expiry time
  • Custom (application) properties — all key-value pairs your producer attached
  • Message body — auto-formatted as JSON if applicable, with a copy-to-clipboard button

The delivery count is especially useful — if it's at the max delivery count for the entity (usually 10), you know the message was dead-lettered due to repeated processing failures.

Step 4: Resubmit or Clean Up

Once you've fixed the underlying bug, you have two options:

  • Resubmit (single) — Click Resubmit in the message detail modal to move one message back to the active queue
  • Resubmit All — Click Resubmit All in the sidebar to move every DLQ message back. This runs as a background task so you can continue working.

If the messages are stale and no longer needed, use Purge to clear the entire DLQ. Bussin will ask for confirmation first.

Step 5: Move Messages to DLQ

Sometimes you need to move active queue messages to the DLQ — for example, to quarantine a batch of poison messages that are causing consumer crashes. In Bussin, open a message from the active queue and click Move to DLQ.

Receive & Lock for Granular Control

For more precise operations, use the Receive & Lock feature. Lock up to 100 messages at once, inspect them, and then choose per-message: Complete (delete), Abandon (release lock), or Dead Letter. This is ideal for selectively cleaning up a queue without affecting healthy messages.

⚠️ Session-enabled entities
Resubmit, purge, and single-message delete operations are not supported for session-enabled entities in the current version. This is a limitation of the peek-lock model when sessions are involved.

title: Deep Search: Scouring Millions of Messages in the Browser
description: Learn how Bussin bypasses standard peeking limits to scan up to 1 million messages in the background without locking your UI.
content:
Standard peeking in Azure Service Bus is limited, often capping out around 10,000 messages. But what if your Dead-Letter Queue has 500,000 messages and you need to find one specific trace ID? That's where Deep Search comes in.

How Deep Search Works

When you add a search filter (Message ID, Subject, or Body content) in Advanced Peek and the entity has more than 10,000 messages, Bussin automatically suggests Deep Search Mode. This launches an intelligent search as a background task that iterates through up to 1 million messages to find your match.

  1. Open Advanced Peek (the gear icon ⚙ next to Peek)
  2. Enter a filter — for example, a MessageId or a specific "customerId": 123 snippet in the JSON body
  3. If the entity has >10K messages, you'll see the Deep Search option prompted automatically
  4. Click Start Deep Search

Tracking progress

Unlike old desktop tools that lock up your interface while they poll APIs, Bussin runs the search fully in the background using a web worker model. You can:

  • See real-time scanning progress in the Running Tasks panel (bottom-left)
  • Continue using other parts of the app — peek other queues, send messages, or organize your namespace
  • Cancel the search gracefully at any time
  • View results instantly when it completes — Bussin shows the matching sequence numbers and provides direct links to jump to those payloads

Filter options

All three search filters use case-insensitive matching locally in your browser memory for maximum speed:

  • Message ID: Exact or partial match on the broker-assigned or custom message ID
  • Subject: Search the Subject/Label metadata field
  • Body Content: Search anywhere inside the raw message body string
🔍 Practical example
Your producer sends order events with a payload containing "CorrelationId": "ORD-999". A customer reports their order wasn't processed. Use Deep Search with a body filter for "ORD-999" to scan the massive Dead Letter Queue, verify its content, and instantly determine exactly why it failed based on its delivery count or metadata error reason.

title: Sending & Scheduling Messages with Bussin
description: How to send test messages, schedule delayed delivery, set broker properties, add custom properties, and batch-send from JSON files — all from the browser.
content:
Sending a Single Message

Click Send in the actions sidebar to open the message composer. Type or paste your message body — Bussin auto-detects whether it's JSON or plain text and sets the ContentType accordingly.

Broker Properties

Expand the Broker Properties accordion to set any of the following:

  • Session ID — required for session-enabled entities, optional otherwise
  • Message ID — auto-generated if left empty
  • Correlation ID — useful for request/reply patterns and tracing
  • Subject / Label — a secondary label for topic subscription filters
  • Reply To and To — routing hints for your consumers
  • Time to Live — override the entity's default TTL (in seconds)
  • Content Type — auto-detected from the body, but overridable

Scheduled Messages

Service Bus supports scheduled enqueue — the message is accepted immediately but only becomes visible to consumers at the scheduled time. Bussin offers two ways to schedule:

Absolute datetime

Use the Scheduled Enqueue (UTC) datetime picker to select an exact date and time. Bussin validates that the time is in the future.

Relative time

Use the Relative Schedule field for quick scheduling. Type a duration like:

  • 30s — 30 seconds from now
  • 5m — 5 minutes from now
  • 2h — 2 hours from now
  • 1d — 1 day from now

The relative time takes precedence over the absolute datetime if both are specified.

Custom Application Properties

Expand the Custom Properties accordion to add key-value pairs that your consumer can read. Each property has a type selector:

  • String — plain text
  • Number — parsed as a numeric value
  • Boolean — true or false

Add as many properties as needed. These are perfect for routing metadata, versioning, or debugging context.

Batch Send from JSON

Switch to the Batch Send tab to upload a JSON file. The file must be a JSON array — each element becomes a separate message body. Bussin shows a preview of the first 10 messages and a count of the total.

Example file:

["Order 1", {"orderId": 123, "status": "pending"}, "Order 3"]

Files up to 5 MB are supported. This is invaluable for replaying test scenarios or seeding a queue with sample data.

💡 Tip: Replaying dead-letter messages
Combine this with the dead-letter workflow: peek DLQ messages, export them, fix the payload, and batch-send the corrected versions back to the active queue.

title: Organizing Namespaces: Favorites, Folders & Multi-Tenant
description: How to bookmark namespaces, organize them into drag-and-drop folders, rename them, and switch between Azure AD tenants — all persisted in your browser.
content:
Favorites

After opening a namespace, you can add it to your Favorites sidebar. Favorited namespaces appear in the left navigation panel for one-click access — no more searching through the full namespace list every time.

All favorites are stored in your browser's local storage. There's no server-side account, so your workspace stays private and works offline.

Folders

As your list of favorites grows, use folders to organize them. Click New Folder at the bottom of the sidebar, give it a name, and start dragging namespaces into it.

Drag and drop

Bussin supports full drag-and-drop for organizing your sidebar:

  • Drag namespaces between folders — move a namespace from one folder to another
  • Reorder namespaces within a folder by dragging them above or below each other
  • Reorder folders — drag folders to rearrange their order
  • Nest folders — drag a folder onto another to create a hierarchy

Every change is saved instantly to local storage.

Rename namespaces

By default, Bussin shows the namespace's Azure resource name. If you work with namespaces like sb-prod-euw-001 and sb-staging-euw-002, you can rename them to friendlier names like "Production Europe" and "Staging Europe". The original hostname is still shown below as a subtitle.

Multi-Tenant Support

Many organizations have multiple Azure AD tenants — for example, a corporate tenant and a client tenant. Bussin's Tenant Selector (in the top bar) lets you switch between all tenants associated with your Microsoft account.

When you switch tenants:

  • Bussin re-authenticates with the new tenant
  • Your namespace list updates to show resources in that directory
  • Your favorites are scoped per tenant — each directory has its own sidebar

This means you can have a completely different workspace for each customer or environment you manage.

Message Statistics

Once inside a namespace, Bussin can pull Azure Monitor metrics directly from the browser. The Stats panel shows:

  • Incoming/Outgoing messages over the last 1, 6, or 24 hours
  • Active messages, Dead-lettered, Scheduled, and Size

This gives you a quick health check without opening the Azure Portal — especially useful when debugging whether messages are being produced or consumed.

💡 Tip: Dark mode
Click the moon/sun icon in the bottom-left corner to toggle dark mode. Your preference persists across sessions.
